pattern_gallery.label: Royal Fork — knight forks king and queen
pattern_gallery.explanation: White's knight on c7 simultaneously attacks the Black king on e8 and the Black queen on a8. Black must move the king out of check, losing the queen on the next move.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: The Royal Fork (knight attacks K + Q at the same time) is the most devastating tactical pattern in chess. Watch for any geometry where your knight could land on a square attacking both.

pattern_gallery.label: Pawn Fork — d2-d4 attacks rook AND bishop
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays d4! The pawn now attacks Black's rook on c5 AND Black's bishop on e5 simultaneously. Black can only save one. Pawn forks are the cheapest possible attacker.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Before every pawn move, ask: does this pawn attack two enemy pieces on the diagonal squares ahead? Pawns capture diagonally — a single pawn push can win material.

pattern_gallery.label: Italian Game — White's Bg5 pin on the f6 knight
pattern_gallery.explanation: After 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4 Nf6 4.d3 Bc5 5.Bg5, White's bishop pins Black's knight on f6 to the queen on d8. The knight is functionally frozen — moving it costs the queen.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Relative pins on knights to queens are everywhere in classical openings. Defenses: ...h6 (kick the bishop), ...d6 (preserve the pin geometry), ...Be7 (block the pin).

pattern_gallery.label: Absolute pin — queen frozen on the e-file
pattern_gallery.explanation: Black's queen is pinned to the king by White's rook on the e-file. ABSOLUTE PIN — Black cannot legally move the queen (would expose the king to check).
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Recognize this geometry instantly. King + valuable piece + opposing rook (or queen, or bishop on diagonal) on the same line = absolute pin. This is why castling early and making a luft matter.

pattern_gallery.label: Bishop Skewer — win the queen
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Bf6+ (or any bishop check on the long diagonal). The Black king on d3 must move out of check. Once the king moves, the bishop captures the queen on h1 behind it.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Skewer = pin in reverse. Higher-value piece in front (king, must move), lower-value behind (queen, gets captured). Long-range pieces (bishop, rook, queen) on long lines = skewer territory.

pattern_gallery.label: Rook Skewer — endgame win
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Rh1+ — wait, the rook is already on h1. White's rook attacks Black's king on d1. Black king must move, exposing Black's rook on a1 to capture.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Rook skewers along ranks and files dominate endgames. King exposed on the back rank = vulnerability. Always look for skewer geometry in simplified positions.

pattern_gallery.label: Discovered Attack — bishop moves, queen attacks
pattern_gallery.explanation: White's bishop on g5 is in front of the queen's diagonal to d5 (Black's queen). If the bishop moves anywhere safely (e.g. Bxf6), it reveals the queen's attack on Black's queen. Discovered attack wins material.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Stack pieces on a line (bishop + queen on a diagonal, rook + knight on a file). Move the front piece for free → reveal the attack from behind. Most powerful when the moving piece ALSO creates its own threat.

pattern_gallery.label: Légal's Mate setup — discovered idea (pre-sacrifice)
pattern_gallery.explanation: After ...Bxd1?? in Légal's trap. White has just played Bxf7+. Now after King moves to e7, Nd5 is checkmate — knight check + discovered mate from bishop. The most famous discovered-attack-into-mate pattern.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Discovered checks are forcing. Combined with a mate threat from the moving piece's destination = forced sequence. Memorize Légal's Mate (1750!) as the canonical pattern.

pattern_gallery.label: Classic Back-Rank Mate — rook delivers
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Re8#. The Black king on g8 is trapped by its own pawns on f7, g7, h7 — no escape squares. Rook on the 8th rank delivers checkmate. The defining mate pattern of millions of games.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: After both sides castle, the back rank becomes vulnerable. Defense: make a luft (push h6/h3 for an escape square). Offense: scan for opponent's blocked king every move with major pieces.

pattern_gallery.label: Back-Rank with defender — sacrifice to remove
pattern_gallery.explanation: Black's rook on a8 defends the 8th rank. White plays Re8+! Rxe8 (forced — king has no moves), but White has set up the position for a follow-up battery in real games. Setup demonstrates removing the defender via trade.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: When the back rank has ONE defender, look for ways to remove it (capture, deflect, pin). Removing the defender = back rank becomes mateable.

pattern_gallery.label: King + Queen Mate — knight opposition delivers
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Qg7# (or Qh7#). The White king on g6 supports the queen, the Black king on h8 has no escape. Classic K+Q mate where the queen lands on a square defended by the king, attacking the cornered king.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: K+Q mate technique: use knight opposition to box the king into a corner, leave 2 squares for shuffling, march your king up to support, deliver the ONE check that ends it. Never give early checks.

pattern_gallery.label: King + Rook Mate — direct opposition delivers
pattern_gallery.explanation: White plays Ra8#. The White king on g6 covers g7 and h7 (Black king's only potential escape squares). The rook delivers mate on the 8th rank. The kings are in direct opposition, then rook check = mate.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: K+R mate technique: rook cuts a file/rank, your king uses direct opposition to push enemy king to the edge, then rook check = mate. Use waiting moves (slide rook on the cut file) to force enemy king into opposition.

pattern_gallery.label: Direct Opposition — kings face one square apart
pattern_gallery.explanation: Kings on d3 and d5 face each other one square apart, with White's pawn on d2. WHITE TO MOVE means Black has the opposition — White's king must yield (move sideways), Black king can advance to the key squares c4/d4/e4 to block the pawn.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: The player NOT to move has the opposition. In K+P endings, this determines who can promote. Count parity carefully — sometimes triangulate to switch who has the move.

pattern_gallery.label: Outflanking — king escorts the pawn
pattern_gallery.explanation: White's king on e6 is in front of the pawn AND has the opposition (Black to move after White's first move). The textbook winning K+P endgame: king clears the path, pawn promotes.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Rule for K+P vs K: if the attacking king is IN FRONT of its pawn AND has the opposition, the pawn promotes. Memorize this — it converts countless endgames.

pattern_gallery.label: Three Principles in action — Center + Develop
pattern_gallery.explanation: After 1.e4 e5 2.Nf3, White has placed a pawn in the center (Center ✓) and developed a knight toward the center attacking e5 (Develop ✓). Black has the same. Both are following the opening principles.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Every good opening implements: (1) Center — control with pawns, (2) Develop — knights and bishops out, (3) Castle — king to safety. Pieces in the center see more (knight in center: 8 squares; in corner: 2).

pattern_gallery.label: Reti / KID setup — flexible center principle
pattern_gallery.explanation: After 1.d4 Nf6 2.Nf3, both sides have developed knights toward the center without committing the e/c pawns. Modern flexible opening showing 'develop first, commit later' philosophy.
pattern_gallery.key_lesson: Opening principles are flexible. You don't have to push BOTH central pawns immediately — sometimes 'control' the center via pieces (hypermodern). The principle is intent, not literal pawn placement.

openings_library.name: London System
openings_library.summary: Universal d4 system. Bishop comes out to f4 BEFORE getting blocked. Same plan against almost anything Black plays — Levy's #1 pick for sub-1500.

openings_library.name: Vienna System
openings_library.summary: Levy's e4 weapon — 70% personal win rate. Sets up the Vienna Gambit (3.f4) against ...Nf6. Off the beaten path; opponents rarely know it.

openings_library.name: Alapin Sicilian (anti-Sicilian for White)
openings_library.summary: Sidesteps 90% of Sicilian theory by playing 2.c3 instead of 2.Nf3. Prepares d4. Removes opponent's prep advantage.

openings_library.name: Italian Game
openings_library.summary: Classical e4 opening, named after 17th-c. Italian master Greco. Bishop aims at f7 (Black's weakest square). Solid, principled.

openings_library.name: Ruy López (Spanish Opening)
openings_library.summary: The most-studied opening at master level. Bishop attacks the c6 knight defending the e5 pawn. Carlsen's go-to — but theory-heavy.

openings_library.name: Queen's Gambit
openings_library.summary: Classic d4 opening offering the c-pawn. Black usually declines (QGD). Got mainstream attention via the Netflix series.

openings_library.name: Caro-Kann Defense
openings_library.summary: Levy's PERSONAL favorite — beat 100+ GMs with it. Building-block defense (c6 supports d5). Solid pawn structure. Drops White's win rate from 51% to 47% at sub-1600.

openings_library.name: Scandinavian Defense
openings_library.summary: Force White to react on move 1. Queen out early (the exception that proves the rule). Memorize: Qd5 → Qa5 → c6 (queen escape).

openings_library.name: Sicilian Defense
openings_library.summary: Most popular Black response at GM level. Asymmetric counter-attack. Theory-dense (Najdorf, Dragon, Sveshnikov, etc.) — Levy doesn't recommend for sub-1500.

openings_library.name: French Defense
openings_library.summary: Building-block sister to Caro-Kann (e6 supports d5 instead of c6). Creates closed positions. Light-squared bishop is the problem child.

openings_library.name: Queen's Gambit Declined (setup)
openings_library.summary: Levy's pick vs d4 (and other slow openings). Decline the c4 pawn — accepting bumps White's win rate to 56%. Setup transposes to London, English, Reti.

openings_library.name: King's Indian Defense (KID)
openings_library.summary: Setup-based defense playable against most White openings. Kasparov's favorite. Counterpunching style — closed positions, kingside pawn storms.
